concepts.lesson: Environment is our basic life support system. It provides the air we breathe, the water we drink, the food we eat, and the land where we live. It is a combination of natural and human-made phenomena. Whatever we see in our surroundings—people, places, things, and nature—constitutes the environment.
concepts.real_example: The school building, the chairs in your classroom, the open field, and the air you breathe are all parts of your environment.
key_points: Combination of natural and human-made elements

concepts.lesson: The natural environment consists of two main components: Biotic and Abiotic. Biotic refers to the world of living organisms, such as plants and animals. Abiotic refers to the world of non-living elements, such as land, water, and air. Both interact with each other to maintain the balance of nature.
concepts.real_example: In a pond, the fish and plants are biotic components, while the water and rocks are abiotic components.
key_points: Both are part of the natural environment

concepts.lesson: The lithosphere is the solid crust or the hard top layer of the earth. It is made up of rocks and minerals and covered by a thin layer of soil. It is an irregular surface with various landforms such as mountains, plateaus, plains, and valleys. It provides us forests, grasslands for grazing, and land for agriculture and human settlements.
concepts.real_example: The ground you walk on and the soil used by farmers to grow crops are part of the lithosphere.
key_points: Solid top layer of the earth
Provides land for various human activities

concepts.lesson: The domain of water is referred to as the hydrosphere, comprising various sources like rivers, lakes, seas, and oceans. The atmosphere is the thin layer of air that surrounds the earth. The gravitational force of the earth holds the atmosphere around it. It protects us from the harmful rays and scorching heat of the sun.
concepts.real_example: The rain falling from the sky (Hydrosphere) and the air that protects us from UV rays (Atmosphere).
key_points: Atmosphere acts as a protective shield

concepts.lesson: Plant and animal kingdom together make biosphere or the living world. It is a narrow zone of the earth where land, water, and air interact with each other to support life. Life exists only in this zone because it provides the necessary conditions for survival.
concepts.real_example: A forest where animals breathe air, drink water from streams, and live on land is a part of the biosphere.

concepts.lesson: An ecosystem is a system formed by the interaction of all living organisms with each other and with the physical and chemical factors of the environment in which they live. All these are linked by transfer of energy and material. There could be an ecosystem of a large rainforest, grassland, desert, or even a small pond.
concepts.real_example: A pond ecosystem where insects, frogs, and fish interact with the water and sunlight.
key_points: Can be as large as an ocean or as small as a puddle

concepts.lesson: Human beings interact with the environment and modify it according to their needs. Early humans adapted themselves to the natural surroundings and led a simple life. With time, requirements grew and humans learned new ways to change the environment, such as growing crops, domesticating animals, and starting industries.
concepts.real_example: Building roads, bridges, and cities by clearing land or using natural resources.

concepts.lesson: A perfect balance is necessary between the natural and human environment. Humans must learn to live and use their environment in a harmonious way. Over-exploitation of resources and pollution can disturb this delicate balance, leading to problems like climate change and loss of biodiversity.
concepts.real_example: Planting trees to balance the carbon dioxide released by factories and vehicles.
